key_insight: Macroscopic elastic loading can drive abnormal grain growth by favoring grains whose orientation reduces elastic energy, especially in ultrafine-grained Ni thin films.
engineering_relevance: This suggests grain stability in thin-film and microstructured metals may depend not only on temperature and plasticity but also on elastic anisotropy and loading direction.
method: Phase-field simulations of grain evolution under cyclic elastic loading
Micromechanical analysis of stress states and grain geometry effects
Comparison across systems with varying elastic anisotropy
evidence_signal: The abstract reports simulation results that reproduce the experimentally observed orientation-selective AGG and identify elastic-energy reduction as the driving force.
watch_item: The framework is mechanistic but still depends on model assumptions about elastic anisotropy, local geometry, and stress fields, so calibration to specific materials is important.
paper_title: Phase-field modeling of elastically driven abnormal grain growth
